original_abstract:
The rapid advancement of pre-trained language models (PLMs) has established a clear trend: scaling up model size consistently leads to superior performance. However, as these models reach massive scales, the conventional paradigm of full-parameter fine-tuning becomes prohibitively expensive and practically infeasible due to immense computational and storage demands. This has catalyzed a shift toward parameter-efficient adaptation, where only a tiny fraction of parameters is optimized while the bulk of the model remains frozen. 

In this paper, we consolidate these diverse strategies under the unifying term "delta-tuning"—using the mathematical notation for change ($\delta$) to represent the sparse set of parameters modified during adaptation. We provide a formal problem definition and a comprehensive taxonomy to categorize existing methods, highlighting their structural similarities and divergence. Beyond empirical observation, we explore the theoretical foundations of delta-tuning, interpreting its success through the lenses of optimization theory and optimal control. To validate these frameworks, we conduct a large-scale empirical study across more than 100 natural language processing tasks, investigating the nuances of efficiency, stability, and performance. Our findings provide both theoretical insights and practical guidelines for the effective adaptation of large-scale PLMs.
humanized_abstract:
Pre-trained language models (PLMs) in recent years have been developing at a very fast pace. And it is widely accepted that larger models lead to better performance. However, as the models grow in size, fine-tuning all parameters of the model becomes too expensive and not practical due to the large amount of computational resources as well as storage space required. Hence, parameter efficient few-shot fine-tuning of large pre-trained language models has become a new trend where only a few percent of the total number of parameters are updated by fine-tuning, while the majority of the weights are kept unchanged.

Recent advances in pre-trained language models (PLMs) have made parameter adaptation a ubiquitous technique in natural language processing (NLP). However, these approaches are scattered and appear to be ad hoc. In this paper, we unify these approaches by naming them delta-tuning (denoted as $\delta$), which refers to the set of changes (parameters to be updated and their corresponding updates) in the PLM. We formally define the optimization problem and present a taxonomy to classify all the delta-tuning based methods. We analyze the theoretical roots of the success of delta-tuning from the perspectives of optimization theory and optimal control. Experimental results are provided to support our theoretical analysis by evaluating the performance of various delta-tuning based methods on 133 NLP tasks. Experimental results demonstrate the effectiveness of delta-tuning based methods in terms of efficiency, stability and model performance.

Recent advances in pre-trained language models (PLMs) have made parameter adaptation a ubiquitous technique in natural language processing (NLP).
